1: My son, do not forget my teaching and let your heart keep my commandments — covenant memory and the heart. The new covenant promise directly addresses this: 'I will put my law within them, and I will write it on their hearts' (Jeremiah 31:33). What Proverbs calls the student to do by effort — keep the commandments in the heart — the new covenant effects by the Spirit. Christ is the mediator of the new covenant (Hebrews 9:15) who makes the internal keeping possible by giving the Spirit who writes the law on the heart.
2: For length of days and years of life and peace they will add to you — the promise of abundant and peaceful life as the fruit of wisdom. Jesus declares: 'I came that they may have life and have it abundantly' (John 10:10). The length-of-days promise of Proverbs is a temporal shadow of the eternal life Christ offers; the 'peace' anticipated here becomes the 'peace that surpasses all understanding' (Philippians 4:7) given by the risen Christ who says 'Peace be with you' (John 20:19-21).
3: Let not steadfast love and faithfulness forsake you; bind them around your neck; write them on the tablet of your heart — the covenant character pair ḥesed (steadfast love) and ʾemet (faithfulness). These are the two attributes YHWH declares at Sinai (Exodus 34:6-7) and the character Christ embodies: 'full of grace and truth' (John 1:14 — charis and alētheia as the Greek equivalent of ḥesed and ʾemet). The tablet of the heart (Jeremiah 31:33; 2 Corinthians 3:3) becomes the NT's metaphor for the Spirit's work in new covenant formation.
4: So you will find favor and good success in the sight of God and man — wisdom produces honor before both God and people. The narrative statement about Jesus — 'And Jesus increased in wisdom and in stature and in favor with God and man' (Luke 2:52) — is a direct echo of Proverbs 3:4 applied to Jesus. Luke presents Jesus as the embodiment of the ideal son who grows up in wisdom and covenant favor, modeling the path Proverbs prescribes.
5: Trust in the LORD with all your heart and do not lean on your own understanding — the total trust demanded by wisdom. This is the foundational faith posture that the NT fills out in Christ: 'Believe in the Lord Jesus, and you will be saved' (Acts 16:31); 'the righteous shall live by faith' (Habakkuk 2:4; Romans 1:17; Galatians 3:11; Hebrews 10:38). Proverbs 3:5 sets up the alternative to human self-sufficiency; Christ is the trustworthy one in whom that total trust finds its object.
6: In all your ways acknowledge him and he will make straight your paths — divine direction flowing from total acknowledgment of God. The NT's calling to 'pray without ceasing' (1 Thessalonians 5:17) and to 'do everything in the name of the Lord Jesus' (Colossians 3:17) is the concrete form of 'acknowledging God in all your ways.' The straight paths that result are the paths of the kingdom: Christ who is 'the way' (John 14:6) is the one in whom all the crooked places are made straight (Isaiah 40:3-5; Luke 3:4-5).
7: Be not wise in your own eyes; fear the LORD and turn away from evil — the humility that wisdom requires. This verse stands against the self-deception that Paul diagnoses in Romans 12:16 ('never be wise in your own sight') and that Christ confronts in the Pharisees who were 'confident in their own righteousness' (Luke 18:9). The wisdom of God revealed in Christ deliberately confounds the wisdom of the self-sufficient: 'God chose what is foolish in the world to shame the wise' (1 Corinthians 1:27).
8: It will be healing to your flesh and refreshment to your bones — the physical dimension of covenant wholeness. The Hebrew concept of shalom encompasses bodily health as well as relational peace. Christ's healing ministry is the concrete enactment of this wholeness: 'He healed all who were sick' (Matthew 8:16; Isaiah 53:4: he bore our illnesses). The healing that wisdom's way promises in Proverbs 3:8 anticipates the wholeness that Christ's kingdom brings — inaugurated in his earthly ministry and consummated in the resurrection of the body.
9: Honor the LORD with your wealth and with the firstfruits of all your produce — the priority offering that acknowledges YHWH as the source of all good. Jesus reinforces this with 'Seek first the kingdom of God and his righteousness, and all these things will be added to you' (Matthew 6:33). The firstfruit principle points forward to Christ himself: 'Christ has been raised from the dead, the firstfruits of those who have fallen asleep' (1 Corinthians 15:20) — the firstfruit offering becomes a type of the resurrection.
10: Then your barns will be filled with plenty and your vats will be bursting with new wine — covenant blessing in response to faithful stewardship. The abundant harvest and overflowing wine connect to the messianic feast imagery: 'the mountains shall drip sweet wine' (Amos 9:13-14; Joel 3:18). Jesus's first sign at Cana — transforming water into superabundant wine (John 2:1-11) — announces that the era of covenant blessing is arriving in him: wisdom's promise of plenty is being inaugurated by the one who is wisdom.
11: My son, do not despise the LORD's discipline or be weary of his reproof — this verse is directly quoted in Hebrews 12:5 as a word of God addressed to believers enduring hardship: 'My son, do not regard lightly the discipline of the Lord, nor be weary when reproved by him.' A direct citation: the word of wisdom spoken in Proverbs becomes the word of God applied to Christians suffering for their faith. Christ himself 'learned obedience through what he suffered' (Hebrews 5:8), modeling the non-despising of discipline that Proverbs calls for.
12: For the LORD reproves him whom he loves, as a father the son in whom he delights — the love behind discipline. This verse is also directly quoted in Hebrews 12:6: 'For the Lord disciplines the one he loves, and chastises every son whom he receives.' A direct citation: Proverbs 3:12 is the theological foundation for the NT's theology of suffering as sonship formation. The Father's love — expressed through disciplinary hardship — is the christological pattern: the Son who suffered did so as the ultimate expression of divine fatherly love shaping him for the work of salvation (Hebrews 2:10: it was fitting that he make the founder of salvation perfect through suffering).
13: Blessed is the one who finds wisdom, and the one who gets understanding — the beatitude of the wisdom-finder. Jesus's Beatitudes (Matthew 5:3-12) echo this form: 'Blessed are those who...' The blessedness that Proverbs 3:13 declares for the wisdom-finder is fulfilled in the Christ-follower: 'Blessed are you, Simon Bar-Jonah! For flesh and blood has not revealed this to you, but my Father who is in heaven' (Matthew 16:17) — to receive wisdom about who Christ is brings blessedness exceeding all other goods.
14: For the gain from her is better than gain from silver and her profit better than gold — wisdom surpasses material wealth. Paul applies this comparative logic to Christ: 'I count everything as loss because of the surpassing worth of knowing Christ Jesus my Lord' (Philippians 3:8). The wisdom tradition's relativization of all material goods in favor of wisdom becomes, in the NT, the relativization of all goods in favor of union with Christ. The 'profit better than gold' is the unsearchable riches of Christ (Ephesians 3:8).
15: She is more precious than rubies and nothing you desire can compare with her — wisdom's incomparable worth. The parable of the merchant seeking fine pearls (Matthew 13:45-46) and the woman who searched for her lost coin (Luke 15:8-10) both image the pursuit of what is incomparably valuable. The pearl of great price and the recovered coin represent Christ's kingdom — and Christ himself — as the one thing of surpassing worth that surpasses everything desired. Wisdom's incomparable value finds its christological expression in the kingdom.
16: Long life is in her right hand; in her left hand are riches and honor — wisdom holds the gifts of the good life. The NT transposes these: the right hand is Christ's position of honor (Hebrews 1:3; Psalms 110:1 — 'sit at my right hand'), and the riches in his hand are 'the riches of his grace' (Ephesians 1:7) and the 'riches of his glory' (Ephesians 3:16). The life and honor that wisdom holds in her hands are held in Christ's hands, and they are given to those united to him.
17: Her ways are ways of pleasantness and all her paths are peace — the character of wisdom's way. Jesus declares himself 'the way' (John 14:6) and gives 'peace that surpasses all understanding' (Philippians 4:7) and 'not as the world gives' (John 14:27). The pleasantness and peace of wisdom's paths are not automatic but flow from union with the one in whom wisdom is embodied. The NT intensification: peace is not merely the absence of conflict but the gift of reconciliation — 'justified by faith, we have peace with God through our Lord Jesus Christ' (Romans 5:1).
18: She is a tree of life to those who lay hold of her; those who hold her fast are called blessed — the third mention of the tree of life in Proverbs (Proverbs 3:18; Proverbs 11:30; Proverbs 13:12; Proverbs 15:4). The tree of life appears first in Eden (Genesis 2:9; Proverbs 3:22) — life's source before the fall — and last in the new Jerusalem (Revelation 22:2: the tree of life yielding twelve kinds of fruit, for the healing of the nations). Wisdom as tree of life is a type: the original gift, lost through disobedience, restored through Christ. Revelation 2:7 promises the tree of life to the one who conquers — the gift of Eden restored in Christ.
19: The LORD by wisdom founded the earth; by understanding he established the heavens — creation through wisdom. This is one of the OT's key texts for the identification of wisdom with the pre-existent Christ. The NT applies this directly: 'All things were made through him' (John 1:3); 'by him all things were created, in heaven and on earth' (Colossians 1:16); 'through whom also he created the world' (Hebrews 1:2). Proverbs 3:19 presents wisdom as God's instrument in creation; the NT identifies that instrument as Christ, the eternal Logos, the divine Son who is wisdom incarnate.
20: By his knowledge the deeps broke open and the clouds drop down the dew — creation's ordering through divine wisdom-knowledge. The same theme continues: the cosmic knowledge that structures creation is the knowledge of the Son. Colossians 1:17 adds: 'in him all things hold together' — Christ is not only the agent of creation but its sustaining principle. The rain and dew that wisdom's knowledge releases in creation are part of the providential ordering that Christ maintains moment by moment.
21: My son, do not lose sight of these — keep sound wisdom and discretion — the call to hold fast to wisdom. The NT's parallel: 'Hold fast to the word of life' (Philippians 2:16); 'hold fast what you have, so that no one may seize your crown' (Revelation 3:11). Holding to wisdom means holding to Christ, and the warning not to lose sight of wisdom is the epistle's warning not to drift from the confession of faith (Hebrews 2:1: we must pay much closer attention to what we have heard, lest we drift away from it).
22: And they will be life for your soul and adornment for your neck — wisdom as the soul's life and beauty. Christ is 'the life' (John 14:6; 1 John 5:12: whoever has the Son has life). The NT's adornment language moves from jewelry to character: 'clothe yourselves with compassion, kindness, humility' (Colossians 3:12); 'let your adorning be the hidden person of the heart with the imperishable beauty of a gentle and quiet spirit' (1 Peter 3:4). Christ adorns the soul with his own character through union with him.
23: Then you will walk on your way securely and your foot will not stumble — the security of the wisdom-path. Jesus's parable ends with the wise man's house standing firm through storm (Matthew 7:24-25). The path-security Proverbs promises is the NT's assurance of God's guarding: 'Now to him who is able to keep you from stumbling and to present you blameless before the presence of his glory with great joy' (Jude 24). Christ is the one who keeps from stumbling — the security Proverbs promises is secured by him.
24: When you lie down you will not be afraid; when you lie down your sleep will be sweet — the peaceful rest of the wisdom-walker. The deep peace of those who trust God is illustrated by Jesus sleeping in the storm (Mark 4:38) — not disturbed by what terrifies his disciples. The promise of sweet sleep anticipates 'the peace of God, which surpasses all understanding, will guard your hearts and your minds in Christ Jesus' (Philippians 4:7). Trust in God through Christ produces the rest that wisdom promises.
25: Do not be afraid of sudden terror or of the ruin of the wicked when it comes — the wisdom-walker's freedom from existential fear. The NT: 'God gave us a spirit not of fear but of power and love and self-control' (2 Timothy 1:7); 'perfect love casts out fear' (1 John 4:18). The freedom from sudden terror that wisdom offers is the fruit of trust in God's sovereignty — and Christ's resurrection is the ultimate argument against existential fear: he has overcome death, the most catastrophic of all sudden terrors (Hebrews 2:14-15).
26: For the LORD will be your confidence and will keep your foot from being caught — divine confidence and keeping. The application in Hebrews 13:6 ('The Lord is my helper; I will not fear; what can man do to me?', citing Psalms 118:6) is the NT's use of this confidence theme. Christ himself is the one who keeps from being caught: 'I give them eternal life, and they will never perish, and no one will snatch them out of my hand' (John 10:28). The keeper who guards from traps is the Good Shepherd.
27: Do not withhold good from those to whom it is due when it is in your power to do it — the immediate enactment of neighbor-love. Jesus's summary of the law — love your neighbor as yourself (Matthew 22:39) and the parable of the Good Samaritan (Luke 10:25-37) — make neighbor-love the test of wisdom's possession. The 'good' withheld or given is the measure of covenant character; James 2:15-17 makes this explicit: faith without works (without giving good when it is in your power) is dead.
28: Do not say to your neighbor, 'Go and come again, tomorrow I will give it' — when you have it with you — the sin of deferral. James 2:16 exactly mirrors this: if a brother lacks food and one says to them, 'Go in peace, be warmed and filled,' without giving them what is needed, what good is that? James applies Proverbs 3:28 directly to the Christian community: wisdom's ethics of immediacy become the test of genuine faith.
29: Do not plan evil against your neighbor who dwells trustingly beside you — the prohibition against using trust as a weapon. This covenant ethics of trustworthiness runs through the NT's teaching on community life: 'Let no one seek his own good, but the good of his neighbor' (1 Corinthians 10:24); 'love does no wrong to a neighbor' (Romans 13:10). Christ himself is the trustworthy neighbor who never plans harm — his hospitality extends to those who will betray him (John 13:26-27), maintaining covenant love even toward the enemy.
30: Do not contend with a man for no reason when he has done you no harm — the prohibition against gratuitous strife. The beatitude 'Blessed are the peacemakers, for they shall be called sons of God' (Matthew 5:9) is the positive form of this wisdom principle. Christ models it in his response to accusers: 'He was oppressed and afflicted, yet he opened not his mouth' (Isaiah 53:7; 1 Peter 2:23). The avoidance of groundless contention is wisdom; Christ's silence before his accusers is its ultimate expression.
31: Do not envy a man of violence and do not choose any of his ways — the prohibition against admiring power obtained through force. The NT confrontation with worldly power is christological: 'the rulers of this age... would not have crucified the Lord of glory' if they had understood wisdom (1 Corinthians 2:6-8). Christ's kingdom is 'not of this world' (John 18:36) and does not operate through violence; Paul declares 'the weapons of our warfare are not of the flesh' (2 Corinthians 10:4). Envy of violent power is choosing the way of the anti-kingdom.
32: For the devious person is an abomination to the LORD but the upright are in his confidence — God's intimate relationship with the upright contrasted with his abhorrence of the devious. Christ makes this intimacy central: 'No longer do I call you servants... but I have called you friends' (John 15:15). The 'confidence' (Hebrew sod, intimate counsel) that God shares with the upright is the intimacy of friendship — disclosed fully in Christ, who reveals the Father's heart to his disciples (John 1:18: the only Son, who is at the Father's side, has made him known).
33: The LORD's curse is on the house of the wicked but he blesses the dwelling of the righteous — the reversal of covenant curses and blessings. The NT's great reversal: Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us — for it is written, cursed is everyone who is hanged on a tree (Galatians 3:13; Deuteronomy 21:23). The curse on the house of the wicked is absorbed by Christ on the cross; the blessing of the righteous dwelling flows from his bearing of the curse. Proverbs 3:33 is the covenant structure; the cross is its resolution.
34: Toward the scornful he is scornful but to the humble he gives grace — this verse is directly quoted twice in the NT. James 4:6 and 1 Peter 5:5 both cite the LXX form: 'God opposes the proud but gives grace to the humble.' A direct application: Peter uses it to call the church to humble submission to God and resistance to the devil (1 Peter 5:5-9); James uses it to call the double-minded to single-hearted devotion (James 4:6-10). The principle of Proverbs 3:34 becomes the theological foundation for the NT's call to humility — the way of Christ, who 'humbled himself' (Philippians 2:8) and to whom God gave the name above every name (Philippians 2:9).
35: The wise will inherit honor but fools get disgrace — the eschatological reversal in which wisdom vindicates its adherents. The Beatitudes' promise of inheritance (Matthew 5:5: the meek inherit the earth) and honor (Matthew 5:12: your reward is great in heaven) are the NT's development of this theme. The final reversal — honor for the wise, disgrace for the fool — is the eschatological verdict of the last judgment, where 'those who honored me I will honor, and those who despise me shall be lightly esteemed' (1 Samuel 2:30). Christ's vindication at the resurrection is the guarantee that wisdom's way leads to the honor Proverbs promises.
